data.title: CNYA: Betting On The Next Stage In China's Economic Development
data.description:
Investment thesis: The efforts to try to contain China's technological rise, initiated during Trump's first presidential term, seem not only to be failing but are arguably backfiring. The creation of a bottleneck effect by restricting China's access to high-end semiconductors and preventing it from gaining the ability to produce such chips domestically created some initial short-term setbacks, but it arguably accelerated China's drive for technological independence from the Western-dominated ecosystem. As a result, China's tech sector is set to grow significantly in the coming years, potentially providing a boost to China's economy and its stock markets. The iShares MSCI China A ETF (BATS:CNYA) offers broad exposure to all the sectors of China's economy, and it is currently my main choice of exposure to the Chinese economy. I am currently looking for more favorable entry points as an opportunity to add to my existing position.

About the ETF

The CNYA ETF offers broad exposure to the Chinese economy.

It is not a tech-specific ETF, but rather an ETF that I see as being well-placed to profit from the effect of what I think is an impending tech boom in China on its overall economy. I will explain later in the article the reasons why I prefer to approach it this way instead of a more tech-specific ETF option, such as the Invesco China Tech ETF (CQQQ), which I have a more limited exposure to.

Other factors of note that are relevant include the expense ratio of .6%, which is more than covered by the 2.2% dividend yield. The fund's performance in the first half of the decade reflects a broad number of factors.

As we can see, we are well off from the highs of this decade, which occurred just after the COVID crisis, as China continued to play the role of the world's factory.

Given the current global geopolitical environment, a bet on this ETF is not so much a bet on a revival of that role. It is a bet on the combination of China's industrial capacity, coupled with accelerated technological progress that we see growing evidence of, giving rise to a new era for China's economy, where value-added becomes the focus, which will help China leap from a manufacturing services-driven economy that requires wages to be suppressed to one where high-value work will allow China to lift all wages to high income country levels.

China's technological rise has been accelerated by failed efforts to impede it

The first clear indications that China was not content to merely play the role of the world's largest factory came in the form of its "Made in China 2025" plan. It was originally an ambition for China's domestic companies to become leaders within the current global tech ecosystem, thus moving its economy out of the middle-income category, into a high-income, value-added, knowledge-based economy status.

It did not take very long for Western and other developed world leaders to realize the potential threat that this plan posed for our economies, and the Trump administration decided to preempt the situation in his first term, most notably by taking down Huawei. It also imposed other restrictions on Chinese access to technologies such as equipment to make high-end semiconductors, which remained America's policy during the Biden administration as well.

It was thought that by creating a bottleneck situation, where China's domestic tech companies would be kept from accessing the most advanced Western technologies that serve as crucial inputs in most tech consumer products, China could be prevented from achieving its 2025 goals. To some extent, one could make a surface-level argument that the containment plan worked. As of right now, Western tech companies, together with close allies such as South Korea & Japan, maintain a tight grip on the global tech market. A closer look suggests that the price of delaying China's technological rise is that we are now having to contend with an increasingly independent, second global technological ecosystem, where Western inputs are increasingly being replaced. This is arguably potentially worse in the long term, because our tech industry can eventually become completely shut out from many markets around the world, where the choice will be one or the other ecosystem and related products & services.

The example of Huawei, where out of nowhere, it announced its return to the domestic phone market, where it reclaimed the top spot this year, is emblematic of what is likely to come. Huawei's new phones are Western input restrictions-proofed. In other words, the restrictions put into place, specifically to prevent the company from competing in the phone market with the likes of Apple (AAPL), failed to contain it, and as a result, at least in China, Huawei is beating Apple, with products that are much less dependent on Western technologies than they would have been otherwise.

If Huawei's project to produce China's own domestic high-end lithography machines, mostly drawing on China's domestic supply chains for inputs, will succeed, which we will potentially find out at some point next year if it is the case, it can be argued that China's break with its dependence on Western inputs in its tech sector is coming to an end. It does not mean that China will stop using Western inputs altogether. It means that containment is no longer possible.

China's transition from a manufacturing services economy for foreign companies to one dominated by value-added gains provided by domestic capital

It might be hard for some to understand what is driving China's efforts to become technologically more independent. Big power rivalry watchers will be tempted to assume that a desire to dominate the world is the main driver. Having observed some of the struggles that former communist countries in Europe are having with escaping the middle-income trap, after 35 years of trying, I am more inclined to see this latter problem that these economies share as being the main driver.

The problem, as I see it in Eastern Europe, where average wages are 2-3 times lower compared with wages in some countries like Germany or the Netherlands, is the low rate of domestic capital ownership. Of the roughly 100 EU companies that currently feature on the Global Fortune 500 list of the largest companies, in most years, the former communist part of the EU, with roughly 100 million citizens in total, only has one or two companies represented. Some years, there are none.

The effect that this lack of domestic capital is having is that net profit extraction from these economies leaves them impoverished relative to the countries where the capital hails from. Their economies are geared to sell labor, mostly for manufacturing activities, while the wages earned also go to a great extent on purchasing goods & services produced by foreign firms, which leads to more profit extraction.

The problem of being stuck in the middle-income trap is more acute and more permanent for the countries in the eastern part of the EU than it is for China. The latter has the second-highest number of companies on the Global Fortune 500 list. A great portion of its economy is still dependent on offering the world cheap manufacturing services; however, its companies are also involved in extracting profits from other economies and repatriating those profits back to China. In other words, China's economy is rebalancing relative to its partners and rivals.

The last dependence that China has on foreign capital is related to intellectual property, where it either has to pay patent fees, or it has to buy inputs from foreign companies to a greater degree than is the case the other way around. In some cases, it is simply faced with inputs being withheld, as I already discussed. With this last constraint seemingly about to be overcome, China is set to break the middle-income trap.

Breaking the middle-income trap means that its PPP GDP & nominal GDP values will converge

The easiest way to understand the effect of China moving from a manufacturing powerhouse to a more value-added, knowledge-based economy is to look at the massive gap that all countries following this same model of high GDP growth by keeping their labor and other costs down have between their nominal GDP & their GDP in PPP terms. China's nominal GDP/capita is currently 6.5 times lower compared with that of the United States. However, in PPP terms, it is only about 3 times lower. As China's economy becomes more dependent on value-added inputs and less dependent on offering cheap manufacturing services, it will start closing the GDP/capita gap in nominal terms more so than in PPP terms. This will be achieved less through continued GDP growth and more by allowing its currency to appreciate, thus boosting the buying power within the domestic economy.

Investment implications

The immediate and obvious answer to betting on China's rising technological might would be to seek out a tech-heavy ETF such as CQQQ as the main investment opportunity. Because I believe this to be transformational for the entire Chinese economy, I believe that the best way to gain exposure is through a broad ETF that reflects the Chinese economy. After all, China's tech sector may achieve the building of a second global tech ecosystem, but there are no guarantees that its tech companies will profit a great deal in the process. I currently have a relatively modest position in the CNYA ETF, which I intend to hold as a long-term position. In the event that its share price declines significantly from current levels, I will consider adding to my position.

  • Risks to my thesis.

Because this investment comes with many risks from diverse directions, I intend to keep my exposure to this ETF limited to well under 3% of my total portfolio, considering that I also have a small position in the tech-oriented CQQQ ETF. My reluctance to overexpose my portfolio to China is in part due to geopolitical risk, keeping in mind what happened to Russian investments in 2022. There are also other risks, including worries that China is at risk of a financial crisis linked to its ongoing property market issues. There is also always the risk that its continued technological advances will stall, leaving its economy vulnerable to continued tech sector restrictions.

  • China's economy can no longer be evaluated as a mostly cheap manufacturing-based model.

The CNYA ETF is down about 1/3 from all-time highs. It is, in part, a testament to past exuberance, as everyone needed supplies during the COVID crisis. It is also a sign of the negativity that we find in market perceptions of China's economic future within the context of major markets for its manufactured goods being increasingly closed or hindered by tariffs. US or EU market access is still very important for China's economy, but it is no longer the make-or-break factor that it used to be. China's total exports amounted to $3.6 trillion in 2024, and only about a third of that went to the US or the EU. In other words, just over $1 trillion, or about 5% of its total nominal GDP, is directly dependent on these markets. The recent trade & economic frictions between the West & China are therefore not a definitive reason to be overly bearish on Chinese stocks.

China is about to enter a new economic era, which is geared toward a value-added system that allows for higher wages, higher living standards, and therefore a higher level of domestic consumption. The CNYA ETF, in my view, is a good way to participate in this trend for the long term. There may be some more market negativity on China along the way in the shorter term, until the market comes to terms with the new realities of China's economy, which I see as potential opportunities to improve my overall bet on my broader China thesis, by adding to my current position, as long as signs are there that my overall thesis remains intact.

data.title: The NextEra In Energy
data.description:
Thesis:

There’s increasing talk that SaaS Software is no longer “eating the world.” Instead, analysts point to the risk of AI native companies threatening the business models of enterprise software firms. I’m recommending investors protect their software-heavy portfolios by diversifying into Utilities via ETFs or individual holdings. This article is the last of three utilities I analyzed for SA with AES (AES) being the “cheapest” and NextEra Energy (NYSE:NEE) being the “best in class.” Dominion Energy (D) could also be “best in class” but it hasn’t executed well, has a “noose over its head” with its offshore wind project and several SA commentators were perturbed over its dividend policy. This analysis focuses on credit quality drivers, particularly as they relate to the Trump Administration’s “ War on Wind.”

Background:

My belief is that SaaS companies will be the next beneficiaries of Artificial Intelligence. However, there already is talk that SaaS companies are at risk to AI native companies and their Agents. While I’m no longer “drinking the cool-aid” that diversification reduces portfolio risk, I believe that a few industry sectors can reduce overall portfolio risk. I believe that fiscal and monetary policies have become a large “factor” explaining most of the long-term movements of equities, gold and cryptocurrencies.

Diversifying into the Utilities Sector

Outside of “physical AI” (i.e., companies that supply the chips, servers and data-centers), I believe diversifying with Utilities is a good risk-mitigating strategy. Utilities stocks could also be beneficial if you desire to stay close to the technology space. Otherwise, the only other sectors one could diversify into are Healthcare and Consumer Staples. (Both of these sectors need to be analyzed carefully given the Trump administrations MAHA initiatives.)

It’s easy to diversify one’s portfolio with utilities as one could simply choose from several low-cost ETFs specializing in this area (see table below). In fact, I think most investors should choose this route. I prefer to buy both the ETF and individual holdings that are underperforming. One reason I do this is that the Utilities sector is more homogenous and regulatory protected than most industries and this lends to my belief that individual companies’ returns will revert to the mean (or industry average). Please refer to my Dominion Energy article for additional information on Utility sector risk and returns.

NextEra Energy

NextEra (NEE) is the largest utility (73 GW of capacity) in the U.S. NextEra operates in the regulated Florida service area via Florida Power & Light (“FPL”). NextEra also holds non-regulated entities under the Energy Resources business segment. If you decide to purchase both NextEra and an ETF such as Utilities Select SPDR (XLU) remember to check the top holdings. For example, XLU’s top holding is NextEra Energy.

NextEra’s Financial Performance

I’ll talk about NEE’s valuation later, but one thing I’ve noticed with companies that trade much higher (and vice versa) than what I had initially expected is that the “market” prices companies efficiently most of the time. In this case, the deeper I dig the larger my confidence is in the company and hence NEE’s “overvaluation.”

2Q’25 earnings continued strong execution with revenues up 10.4% y/y (missing estimates) and Adjusted EPS at $1.05 (beating estimates by $0.04) which was driven by Florida Power & Light and its unregulated business unit (Energy Resources). The evolution of both businesses are in the chart below.

Long-term Growth

Management expects EPS growth of 6-8% from 2024 to 2027. However, I believe they can do better for a few reasons:

  1. they’ve already hinted they’d be surprised if they don’t hit the top end of that range (remember management tends to be conservative),
  2. Hyperscalers continue ramping up estimates for data-center spending,
  3. the company operates in a very regulatory supportive (think high “allowed returns”) environment and
  4. industry consultants have changed their expectations for bottlenecks in data-centers from semiconductors to energy as it's going to take too long to build-out the necessary energy infrastructure (think nuclear power plants, transmission lines…). And remember, the quickest “time to power” is from renewables – of which NextEra is the industry leader.

Most of NextEra’s renewables projects fall under its Energy Resources segment. The timetable is below. As you can see, nearly half (in GW) will be in Solar with the remainder being in Wind and Energy Storage. This table is from 7/25 and the backlog will likely be up another 3GW by next quarter’s reporting period. As a sidenote, NextEra has done a very good job at mitigating risks related to the Trump administration’s “War on Renewables” such as domesticating the supply chain (to reduce tariffs), choosing sites that are mostly on private land and securing U.S. made batteries. (About 20% of Energy Resources’ PPAs (Purchase Power Agreements) are subject to “re-contracting” at higher market rates, providing optionality going forward into 2035, according to NextEra’s September Investor Deck: p22.)

Florida is a top-tier service area

When I was a Utility analyst for a commercial bank, I always favored utilities that had strong natural monopolies and service areas and Florida was amongst the best. Some of its advantages are:

Strong Regulatory Framework: Florida collaborates well with utilities, not only allowing high ROE but the indirect effect is that utilities tend to re-invest more into infrastructure which allows better cost efficiencies and grid reliability in the long-term. Rapid population growth: needless to say, Florida has been growing its population for decades and this is expected to continue (chart). Strong economy: According to S&P Global, Florida’s economy is the third largest and is one of the fastest growing in the country. According to The Tax Foundation, Florida is rated one of the top business climates in the nation. Self-contained electric grid: Given the peninsula shape of the state of Florida, it doesn’t have many connections with other grids such as ISO and thus it has greater control over local grid management and lower competition from outside IPPs.

Seeking Alpha Ratings

SA Analysts rate NextEra a “Buy” (4.00 rating), Wall Street also rates it a “Buy” (3.84) The Quant rating is “Hold” due to NextEra’s valuation and weak stock momentum. Six analysts raised their FY1 earnings estimates during the last 90 days – two have reduced them. Six analysts reduced Revenue estimates and I note that this has been an issue for NextEra for several quarters.

Dividend history

I’ve never written about or been interested in dividends as I rather the tech companies I follow reinvest them into innovative technologies. However, it’s important to highlight NEE’s dividend history as it’s another example of how the company has executed well. NextEra’s dividends have grown at a 10% CAGR over the last 20 years and management expects this to continue at least through 2026. SA rates NextEra A+ for “Dividend Consistency.”

Average Outage Time

This last example shows how a supportive regulatory environment not only allows shareholders higher ROEs but allows NextEra enough capital to harden its grid against storms etc. by making its transmission lines more robust, undergrounding electric distribution system to homes, inspect poles for those that are above-ground and installing real-time flood monitoring systems at substations. This can also be seen in how long a typical outage is for NextEra’s FPL segment compared to peers (which is half that of the typical utility company).

Credit Ratings & Risks

As I said earlier, I believe that NextEra’s service area and regulatory support is the foundation of its strong credit profile (rated A- by S&P). Below you can see that NextEra’s has had a high credit rating of which it maintained throughout a volatile period which included Covid-19. For perspective, NEE’s credit ratings are a notch or two above the two other utilities (AES, Dominion) that I covered.

Key ratings drivers include:

  • Constructive regulatory environment
  • Strong business profile highlighted by having the largest Renewables portfolio in the U.S.
  • Focus on long-term contracts in the non-regulated business
  • Strong backlog
  • Expected improvement in credit metrics
  • Mitigation of Physical Risks (e.g., storms, fires)

The largest risks are similar to what we saw for AES and Dominion – that being concerns over the Trump administration’s rollback of the Inflation Reduction Act and implementation of tariffs, as well as Execution risks related to a large backlog (i.e., high CapEx). However, I believe these risks are manageable. For example, several of NextEra’s renewables projects are “grandfathered” and thus protected by safe-harbor rules. (As a side-note, I caution against examining NextEra’s debt metrics at face-value as they need to be adjusted for non-recourse debt.)

Stock performance

NextEra Energy’s stock has had strong performance for over a decade with the only exception being with the XPLR Infrastructure (XPLR) event that re-rated the stock. During 9/2023, XPLR (fka NextEra Energy Partners) announced sharply lowered growth expectations which effectively caused a one-time sharp drop to NextEra Energy’s share price. NextEra has a controlling interest in XPLR but has since distanced itself from the company and will not provide equity infusions, according to Fitch Ratings.

How strong is Data-Center Demand?

If you’ve read this article, you can see that so far we’ve got a company that’s highly regulated (i.e., safe) that’s executed well both in its business (i.e., storm resistant) and financially (i.e., dividend policy). To top that off, there is HUGE demand for electricity. I’ve written and shown numerous charts in my previous articles and still they don’t do justice as demand growth keeps getting revised upwards. Last time I showed you charts such as McKinsey & Co.’s U.S. data center demand growing at a CAGR of 22%. But this too will need to be revised. Instead, I present the chart below of Hyperscaler Capex Spending growth. These are some of the largest companies in the U.S. which should see flat CapEx spending due to the law of large numbers. Instead, it’s accelerating. I’ve confirmed this trend with others in the space including Coreweave (CRWV) Oracle (ORCL) and Nebius (NBIS).

Repeating what I wrote previously, “ I believe Dominion is well positioned for the tsunami of electricity demand needed for AI training & inference.” You can replace Dominion with NextEra, or any other utility, because demand is that strong – this is not a “ zero-sum game.”

Valuation

NextEra shares have higher valuations compared to AES and Dominion as well as the whole utility sector. NextEra’s forward (Non-GAAP) P/E is just under 20x while the Utility sector trades around 1 8x. However, I believe its higher valuation is justified given NEE’s strong execution and better credit profile. There is a price to pay for “best in class.”

Conclusion

Mega-sized data-center deals are now being announced weekly. When I wrote the Dominion article Oracle reported an astonishing backlog of$455Bn. As I’m writing this, NVIDIA (NVDA) announced a$100Bn deal with OpenAI to build an initial 10GW of data-centers by next year with expectations for over $1 trillion over the next few years.

I’ve provided a chart below so you can get some perspective to exactly how much power that is. Remember…these plants take years to build but the demand is needed now. Given the above and the quality of NextEra, my rating is “ Strong Buy.”

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 238: $23,800 Allocated, $2,580.87 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
This was the week the markets had been waiting for, as the September FOMC meeting finally occurred. Fed Chair Powell delivered his remarks after the Fed reduced rates by 25 bps for the first time in 2025. He disclosed that the Fed participants have indicated that the consensus level for rates in 2025 is 3.6%, and CME Group is pricing in a 74.4% chance we receive another 50 bps of reductions in 2025. The Fed has finally started their cutting process again, and the S&P 500 finished up 0.49% on the week, while the Nasdaq climbed 1.14%. I believe that income-producing assets are going to become very appealing as the 3-month, 12-month, 2-year, and 5-year bonds have all fallen below a 4% yield. As the Fed reduces rates, I believe that capital will move from risk-free assets back into the equity markets, and the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio will do very well. Many companies should see the assets on their books increase in value, and I am excited to see where the market goes from here. After seeing what Microsoft (MSFT) announced about their new data center, I think that the hyperscalers are going to only get bigger, and this should be positive for many of the ETFs and CEFs within the portfolio. As Q3 comes to an end, I am now looking toward Q3 earnings and seeing what the commentary is like on the earnings calls. Now that the cutting cycle is occurring, I'm going to see if my assessment was correct with where I have been allocating capital.

After a 6-week string of positive weeks, this was the first negative week in a month and a half. After allocating $23,800 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 238 at $31,634.42. The account's profitability decreased by -2.31% (-$187.97) to $7,934.42, which is a return on invested capital of 33.48%. This week I added to my positions in NNN REIT (NNN), VICI Properties (VICI), and the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI). The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $23.61 of dividend income in week 238. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $7.19 (0.28%) to $2,580.87. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $4,809.15 of income since its inception, which is 20.21% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio decreased in value this week, but I believe the account is positioned well for the future. It’s going to be interesting to see how the remainder of the year plays out, but I think that there could be close to $2,750 of forward dividend income being generated by the time 2025 comes to an end.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

I have continued to say that eventually there will be some negative weeks for the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, as stocks just don’t go up. A 6-week streak was broken, and that’s quite ok, as the portfolio has proven time and time again that it’s able to meet adversity head-on. I started this portfolio with $100 and added $100 per week toward income-producing investments. I have been documenting starting an income-producing portfolio from the ground up on a weekly budget. My investment goals were to generate recurring income while mitigating downside risk. Capital appreciation has been a secondary goal, and things are going quite well. The portfolio is generating a yield of 8.16%, and when I look at the yield on invested capital, it increases to 10.89%. I am excited to see how the next several years unfold, as there will be a tremendous amount of capital being reinvested back into the portfolio along with the weekly allocations.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $909.36 (35.23%)
  • Equities $585.38 (22.68%)
  • CEFs $416.71 (16.15%)
  • REITs $416.27 (16.13%)
  • BDCs $253.15 (9.81%)

Investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year, as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. After 38 weeks in 2025, $1,629.47 of dividend income was produced, which is 100.39% of the total income from 2024. There were 554 positions that paid a dividend, which is 78.14% of the dividends generated in 2024.

There was $23.61 of dividend income generated this week, which brought the average amount of dividend income I have generated on a weekly basis to $42.88. This is an increase of $11.67 (37.38%) YoY, and there have been 3 weeks that exceeded $100, unlike in 2024, when the largest week was $89.13. I’m really excited for the rest of September to unfold, as the YoY data is showing me a huge trend going up and to the right with dividend income. Over the next several years, I should be able to exceed $5,000 in forward dividend income in this account and basically double my annualized allocation to the portfolio.

There is a week and a half left in September, and I have generated $98.83 in dividend income this month. In March I generated $189.26 of dividend income, and in June I produced $195.08 in dividend income. I am excited to see how the rest of September unfolds, as it’s the last month in Q3. Based on how the previous 2 quarters have scaled, I believe that I will exceed $200 of dividend income this month. I am also starting to believe that I will not drop below $200 of monthly income going forward after September is closed out. I am excited to see how the chart below finishes the year, as I should be positioning myself to generate close to $2,700 of forward dividend income walking into 2026.

My long-term goal is to have 75% or more of the portfolio generate at least 1 share per year from their dividend income. There are currently 44 positions accomplishing this, which is emphasized by the green section of the table below. There is $448.29 of forward dividend income being generated from the new dividends being generated from all the positions generating at least 1 share from their dividends annually. Many positions are still lagging, but I am making a strong effort allocating capital toward them so I can get at least 50% of the portfolio to accomplish this goal by the end of the year. My fixation on this is because it will allow me to advance the compounding impacts from the dividend snowball, and it also makes sure that I am not investing in the same positions each week.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

ETFs have officially reached 20% of the portfolio again, and I added to REITs heading into the rate cut. It’s difficult for me to stay away from these sectors, as I believe that technology is going to heat up throughout earnings season and that there are still a lot of deals in the REIT segment. Individual equities now represent 39.11% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio while generating 22.68% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 60.89% of the portfolio and generate 77.32% of the forward income. I am working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5–10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

Altria Group (MO) is starting to make its way back down to a more realistic level compared to the rest of the portfolio. It’s going to take a while, maybe not until the middle of 2026, but I would like to see Realty Income (O) and Enbridge (ENB) below 3% and MO under 4% of the portfolio. One of the keys to this portfolio’s success has been the level of diversification. Eventually, the top 3 positions will get smaller, and I will feel more comfortable allocating capital toward them. I am expecting the top 20 positions to change a bit next year, and I am hoping that I can get all the positions a bit more even in the future. While I have allowed myself a 5% maximum, I really want to have a well-rounded portfolio that can mitigate risk well.

SL Green (SLG) has been replaced by the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF in the top 10 positions. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,642.19, and they finished the week with a value of $9,272.25. This is an ROI of $2,630.55 (39.60%). There has been $1,210.91 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 18.23% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $645.28 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 9.71%. These positions represent 29.31% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 25% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 238 Additions

This week I added to NNN REIT, VICI Properties, and the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund.

NNN REIT

  • NNN is one of the REITs that I don’t own enough of, as it's not producing 1 share per year from its dividends yet
  • NNN has a dividend yield of 5.7% and has increased its dividend for more than 3 decades
  • I wanted to add to NNN before the rate cuts started, as I believe their book of assets will increase in value as rates decline

VICI Properties

  • VICI is a similar story to NNN, and I recently wrote an article about VICI outlining my investment thesis (can be read here)
  • VICI is another REIT I want to own more of, as it owns the land and buildings that many casinos operate, including Caesars
  • VICI has a dividend yield of 5.64% and has increased the dividend for the past 6 years
  • VICI’s Q2 results showed strong revenue and AFFO growth, with management raising guidance

PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund

  • As rates continue to decline, PDI’s book of assets should appreciate in value, causing its NAV to increase, which should correlate to its share price moving higher
  • PDI has been able to maintain a $2.65 distribution, which is a forward yield of 13.36%, and I believe it will continue paying this into a cutting environment
  • I think now is a good time to get bullish on fixed income, as previously issued bonds should increase in value as newly issued debt will have lower coupons attached to them

Week 239 Gameplan

Next week I am probably going to add to ONEOK (OKE), and I am not sure what else I am going to purchase.

Conclusion

Well, we got rate cuts this week, and the markets went a bit higher, but the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio slightly retraced. Despite the initial results, I still believe that the portfolio is positioned well for a lower rate environment, as there are many positions that should theoretically get a boost from lower rates. I’m excited the forward dividend income is making an S-curve, and the powers of compounding will only intensify as more dividend income is generated and then reinvested. When I think about the future, this portfolio could easily offset my living expenses when I decide to retire. I can’t wait to see what the remainder of 2025 looks like and how the portfolio does in 2026. Please leave all of your thoughts in the comment section below, as I enjoy interacting with everyone.

data.title: I Still See Upside In H.B. Fuller's Steady Compounding Story
data.description:
Thesis

Back at the start of summer, I rated H.B. Fuller Company (NYSE:FUL) as a buy. The company got rid of parts of the business that didn’t make much money, shifted more attention to its more profitable adhesive products, and was already seeing better profit margins, stronger cash flow, and a stock price that pointed to EPS growing steadily in the mid-teens percentage range. On top of that, there was room for future dividend increases. To me, it seemed like an obvious addition to my portfolio: the company had trimmed down, could charge better prices, and its stock was reasonably priced.

Since my rating, FUL has been a total dud, roughly down 4% (and still going lower pre-market) on a total return basis, while the S&P 500 (SP500) is up about 8%. Not the outcome I expected in the near term.

With that in mind, after looking at what’s changed and what hasn’t, my research shows H.B. Fuller’s disciplined shift in its portfolio and focus on margins add up to a steady, compounding earnings story that’s still priced below its real quality and direction.

H.B. Fuller Q3 Revenue and Margin Trends

The U.S. economy has been losing some of its steam, and tariffs are keeping material costs high. I’d flag this as especially relevant for H.B. Fuller, which sells adhesives into construction, electronics, and industry. S&P Global’s September flash PMI shows growth slowing for the second straight month while production costs stay up. That’s the backdrop for H.B. Fuller’s third-quarter results.

The company’s net revenue was $892 million, which was 2.8% lower than the same period last year. However, if you adjust for the sale of the Flooring business, revenue was actually up by 1.6%. Prices went up by 1.0%, but the amount of goods sold dropped by 1.9%, which meant organic sales fell by 0.9%. Foreign exchange added a 1.0% benefit, while mergers and acquisitions reduced growth by 2.9%. The biggest help came from offering a better product mix and executing well.

Moving on to profit margins, those improved nicely. Adjusted gross margin rose by 190 basis points to 32.3%. On the expense side of things, Selling, General and Administrative costs were $175 million on a reported basis and $169 million on an adjusted basis, compared to $164 million last year. The CFO noted that after accounting for the effects of mergers and acquisitions, currency fluctuations, and performance-based pay, adjusted expenses were basically flat compared to last year.

The company’s earnings got stronger this quarter. Diluted EPS were $1.22, and adjusted EPS was $1.26, which is 12% higher than before. This growth came from higher adjusted net income and having fewer shares in circulation.

Adjusted EBITDA was $171 million, up 3% from last time. Profit margins improved as well, rising by 1.1 percentage points to 19.1%.On the cost side, interest expenses dropped slightly to $33.6 million from $35.3 million. That helped pre-tax margins improve to 9.3%, up from 7.9%.Gross profit was $285 million. On an adjusted basis, gross profit was $288 million, showing that the margin improvements are clearly carrying through the company’s financial results.

Cash Flow and Leverage Trends

Fuller continued to generate healthy cash. Cash flow from operations grew 13% compared to last year, reaching $99 million in the quarter. Net working capital increased to 17.0% of annualized revenue, which is 0.9 percentage points higher than a year ago. This was mainly because the company held a touch more inventory to support changes in its manufacturing setup.

At the end of the quarter, net debt was $1.96 billion, which is $58 million lower than the previous quarter. The company’s leverage also got better, improving to 3.3x net debt compared to trailing adjusted EBITDA, down from 3.4x in Q2.

Engineering Adhesives came out on top, leading in both growth and profit. Revenue rose 4.7% to $272 million, with adjusted EBITDA hitting $63 million. That’s a 23.3% margin, up 1.9 points. Building Adhesive Solutions barely moved. Revenue rose 2.3% to $234 million, with adjusted EBITDA at $41 million. The margin was 17.7%, up just 0.1 points.

Hygiene, Health and Consumable Adhesives fell 1.0% to $386 million. Margin still widened 50 basis points to 16.9%, with higher prices and efficiency covering weaker volumes. Corporate unallocated revenue dropped because of the flooring sell-off, making the year-over-year comparison cleaner.

Management gave the stock-standard heads up we're used to reading about, maintaining "discipline" in how they run the business. That said, even though customer demand is weaker right now, they highlighted that profit margins are growing and EPS, when adjusted, are still rising by double digits. The company confirmed that over time it's still aiming for an EBITDA margin above 20%.

Fuller now expects its net revenue to fall by 2% to 3% for the year. Organic revenue (which excludes things like acquisitions or divestitures) is expected to be flat to up about 1%. Adjusted EBITDA is pegged between $615 million and $625 million, a 4% to 5% bump. Adjusted EPS is slated for $4.10 to $4.25, up 7% to 11%. Cash from operations should land between $275 million and $300 million, with about $140 million set aside for capex.

FUL’s Valuation

At around $59, FUL's trading at a blended P/E ratio of 14.34, which is below its fair value level of 15.00 on FAST Graphs and well under the longer-term “normal” range of 17.49 to 19.52.

Next, looking ahead, analysts are anticipating that the forward non-GAAP P/E will decline from 14.07 in fiscal year 2025 to 12.61 in 2026 and 10.86 in 2027, while earnings are projected to grow 9.74%, 11.56%, and 16.13% in those years, respectively.

I think that that falling P/E ratio is important: basically, if the multiple simply moves back up to the fair value of 15 (see forecasting chart above), the stock’s fair value would be about $62 compared to today’s $59, and the upside would grow further as earnings continue to increase.

Moving on to a forward non-GAAP basis, FUL's PEG ratio is 0.97, that's compared to 1.39 for the sector and its own five-year average of 1.29 which, I'd argue, would make sense to pay a mid-teens P/E multiple for a company with mid-teens growth.

And on the cash side, that also strengthens the case: the forward price-to-cash-flow is 8.52, which is cheaper than its five-year average of 11.16 and the sector’s 9.24. Meanwhile, the forward EV/EBITDA is 8.31, below both the sector average of 8.72 and its five-year average of 10.05. So just like last quarter, FUL's not looking expensive.

Even so, looking at the risks, the GAAP P/E ratio is somewhat high, with 19.64x forward and 32.23x TTM. This is because restructuring costs and portfolio changes are still counted in GAAP numbers. And that means if the company has problems with its results or operations, the gap between adjusted earnings and reported earnings could widen again. On top of that, I’m not very comfortable with the company’s debt (although, we saw earlier that it is moving lower), with long-term debt making up 51% of its total capital (per FAST Graphs data above), which is why it only has a BB credit rating. Overall, H.B. Fuller Company needs to keep generating steady cash flow.

Having said that, right now, its TTM price-to-cash-flow is 13.85, which is higher than the sector median.

Final Takeaway

I’m keeping my (long-term) buy rating. All things considered, EPS keeps growing, cash flow is healthy, and FUL's valuation multiple is still below what I think is its fair range. The Engineering Adhesives division is driving the company’s growth. And at the same time, selling off less useful parts of the business and keeping expenses in check are very helpful in moving that EBITDA toward the 20% target. Put together, this creates the kind of steady momentum you want to see from a company that looks capable of delivering earnings growth in the mid-teens.

One final note: the pre-market selling and overall momentum suggest that we might soon see the RSI in the 30s. That’s going to test, and in my view eventually break, the current parallel channel. Once that happens, there’s a strong indication we could see the low 50s here, maybe even bring a new trading range within the 45–50 zone.

data.title: AstraZeneca: Oncology Breakthroughs And Farxiga Surge Excite Investors
data.description:
Just over four months have passed since my last article on AstraZeneca (NASDAQ:AZN) (OTCPK:AZNCF) "Why AstraZeneca May Be 2025's Most Underrated Pharma Stock," and during that time, its share price has risen 6%.

In it, I presented an analysis of clinical data for its PCSK9 inhibitor, laroprovstat, the prospects for its GLP-1 franchise, which includes AZD9550/AZD6234 and AZD6234, and the reasons behind its strong financial results for Q1 2025.

From a technical analysis perspective, a correction began on September 2, when AstraZeneca's stock price reached $82. In my opinion, it will continue until the gap [$75.2-$76.1] closes and it reaches a strong support level located near the VWAP, namely $74.02, after which the impulse phase within wave 5 will resume up to the resistance level of $85.

But before I discuss AstraZeneca's financial results for Q2, which reinforced my confidence that its risk/reward profile remains attractive, I want to ask the following question.

How successful is AstraZeneca in R&D?

In short, it hasn't disappointed me.

Strong clinical data for baxdrostat in the treatment of hypertension

So, among the most recent substantial milestones, I want to highlight AstraZeneca's publication of stunning results from the BaxHTN Phase III study on August 30, 2025, which assessed the efficacy and safety profile of baxdrostat in the treatment of hard-to-control hypertension.

Why did I use the word "stunning"?

Because in this pivotal clinical trial, the addition of baxdrostat to SoC [standard of care] resulted in a reduction in mean placebo-adjusted systolic blood pressure of 9.8 mmHg [P<0.001; 95% CI: from -12.6 to -7.0] and 8.7 mmHg [P<0.001; 95% CI: from -11.5 to -5.8] for the 2 mg and 1 mg doses, respectively, by week 12.

But in my opinion, the efficacy of AstraZeneca's aldosterone synthase inhibitor is more clearly demonstrated by the secondary endpoint.

So, 39.4% of patients in the baxdrostat 1 mg group had controlled systolic blood pressure below 130 mmHg, 40% of those who were in the baxdrostat 2 mg group, and only 18.7% of those who took a placebo plus SoC.

I also analyzed clinical data on its competitors, including Idorsia's Tryvio (OTC:IDRSF) and George Medicines' Widaplik.

And I can say that if baxdrostat is approved by the FDA and EMA, it has a high chance of becoming the preferred choice in the treatment of uncontrolled hypertension, one of the most common cardiovascular disorders affecting approximately 92.9 million Americans, according to HHS' Million Hearts.

Finally, I just want to mention that AstraZeneca estimates peak sales of baxdrostat, which is being developed as monotherapy and in combination with dapagliflozin [SGLT2 inhibitor] for the treatment of CKD [chronic kidney disease] and the prevention of heart failure, will exceed $5 billion.

Let's continue.

Strong sales of its oncology franchise and Farxiga

So, its revenue reached $14.46 billion in Q2 2025, beating the consensus estimate by $362 million.

Overall, it grew 6.6% year-over-year and 6.3% quarter-over-quarter, despite a decline in demand for Nexium, its former blockbuster widely used to treat gastroesophageal reflux disease before Teva Pharmaceuticals (TEVA), Viatris (VTRS), and others launched generics in 2015.

Among its key competitors, I also single out Phathom Pharmaceuticals' Voquezna (PHAT), which is more effective in reducing heartburn symptoms and healing erosive esophagitis.

Overall, its revenues were approximately $39.5 million, up more than 440% from Q2 2024.

In addition, among AstraZeneca's weak-performing medications, I highlight Soliris [eculizumab], which is approved for the treatment of various rare diseases, including myasthenia gravis and paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria.

Its sales were $530 million in Q2 2025, down 24.3% year-on-year due to the launch of its two biosimilars, namely Teva/Samsung Bioepis' Epysqli and Amgen's Bkemv (AMGN).

But as often happens with Big Pharma, AstraZeneca knows how to build its business in such a way as to minimize the damage from the loss of exclusivity of its key medications.

So, Soliris' successor is Ultomiris [ravulizumab], the advantages of which I mentioned in the article "AstraZeneca's Growth Potential: 6 Reasons For A Buy Rating".

Thanks to them, its sales grew by 24.4% year-on-year to reach $1.18 billion for the three months ended June 30, 2025.

I'll continue with an analysis of AstraZeneca's earnings.

So, its non-GAAP EPS beat my upside scenario by 2 cents, reaching $1.09.

It grew by 10.1% year-on-year despite AstraZeneca's R&D costs rising from $3 billion in Q2 2024 to $3.51 billion in Q2 2025.

In my opinion, the increase in its R&D expenses is directly due to the completion of the acquisition of EsoBiotec on May 20 for up to $1 billion and the acceleration of patient enrollment in ongoing Phase III studies, especially those evaluating the efficacy/safety profile of its anti-cancer medications [rilvegostomig, volrustomig, and camizestrant], as well as baxdrostat, which I mentioned earlier.

I have the following logical question.

But thanks to which medications, AstraZeneca's revenue and EBITDA continue to grow at a relatively high rate?

To answer this question, I noted the sales of not only its key drugs, but most of them, to understand which of them could become a trigger for increased activity of short sellers.

So I'll start with AstraZeneca's oncology franchise.

First, its revenue reached about $6.31 billion in Q2 2025, up 18.4% year-on-year, driven mainly by 4 medications, namely Tagrisso, Imfinzi, Calquence, and Enhertu.

So, I want to draw your attention to Imfinzi, which is a PD-L1 monoclonal antibody, whose direct competitors are Merck's Keytruda (MRK), Bristol-Myers Squibb's Opdivo/Opdivo Qvantig (BMY), and Regeneron's Libtayo (REGN).

Sales of Imfinzi were approximately $1.46 billion for the three months ended June 30, 2025, up 26.9% year-over-year and 15.4% quarter-on-quarter, driven primarily by increased demand for its use in the treatment of lung cancer and hepatocellular carcinoma, as well as its FDA approval on March 28 in combination with two chemotherapy drugs [cisplatin+gemcitabine] for the treatment of certain patients with muscle-invasive bladder cancer.

But that's not all.

The important milestone that I highlight is that on July 28, 2025, AstraZeneca announced that the FDA had accepted for review an sBLA for Imfinzi in the treatment of early-stage gastric/gastroesophageal junction cancers based on promising results from the MATTERHORN Phase 3 study.

In addition, the FDA has granted 'Priority Review' to this application, meaning I expect a decision to be made in December 2025.

Why did I use the word "promising"?

Because in this trial [NCT04592913], the group of patients who received the Imfinzi-based perioperative regimen had a 29% reduced risk of disease progression, relapse, or death [event-free survival or EFS in the table below] compared to the group of patients who received chemotherapy alone [HR=0.71, P<0.001], which remains the 'standard of care' for the treatment of these types of stomach cancer.

Overall, according to data provided by the Global Cancer Observatory, the number of annual new cases of stomach cancer in the US alone will increase from 25.5 thousand in 2022 to 36.5 thousand in 2050.

After I discussed the performance of AstraZeneca's key medications, the following logical question arose in my mind.

What risks could damage its balance sheet?

Risks

In the table below, I have provided updated risks ranging from a slowdown in Lynparza sales growth due to increased competition from GSK's Zejula (GSK) and Pfizer's Talzenna (PFE), as well as the fact that many of AstraZeneca's manufacturing facilities are located outside the US [risk from Trump's tariff threats], namely in the UK, Sweden, and China.

Takeaway

Unlike most Big Pharma players, AstraZeneca remains a Wall Street favorite.

Yes, it's not a common choice in portfolios of long-term retail investors. In my opinion, this is mainly due to AstraZeneca's highly diversified portfolio of medications, which lacks one or two blockbusters that would spark heated debates, including here at Seeking Alpha.

More specifically, I'm talking about Pfizer's COVID-19 vaccine, called Comirnaty, and weight-loss drugs including Eli Lilly's Zepbound (LLY) and Novo Nordisk's Wegovy (NVO).

But even despite AstraZeneca's lack of 'flashy superstars', and the fact that it has significant manufacturing capacity outside the US [in Singapore, Sweden, and the United Kingdom], its stock remains largely unaffected by the noise coming from Robert F. Kennedy Jr. or Trump.

In my view, this is due to the strong performance of the medications I discussed earlier in the article, as well as Truqap and Tezspire.

Overall, AstraZeneca expects its core EPS to increase by a low double-digit percentage.

As a result, I continue to cover AstraZeneca with a 'Buy' rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Why Stocks Haven't Crashed (Yet?)
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Markets are hitting all-time highs, while the Fed cuts and the unemployment picture seems to worsen.

While there are hints of recession, AI tech companies haven't gotten the memo and continue to rip higher.

It seems like everyone expects this bubble to pop, but markets keep grinding higher.

And indeed, it seems like under normal circumstances this would be the case.

But there is now a new normal, and this new normal means that the default in markets is up, and the likelihood of a long-lasting bear market is reduced.

That’s not to say it won’t happen, but there are enough mechanisms and interest to keep propping the market up.

We break down the four main ones below.

Government Intervention

Since 2008, and perhaps to a larger extent since COVID, the government has taken over as the main employer and even growth engine of the economy.

In fact, without government spending, GDP growth would be almost non-existent in 2024

Wartime levels of spending have become commonplace in peacetime, and this seems to be true regardless of who’s in power.

The current administration seems intent on running the economy hot, not just through monetary policy, but also through unprecedented fiscal policy, like buying into publicly traded companies.

For better or worse, our economy is increasingly being centrally directed, which ultimately results in fiscal stimulus being pushed into businesses.

Monetary Debasement

This ties into monetary debasement, which is ultimately the force that allows all this to happen. Fiscal spending has no limit as long as we can keep printing and debasing the currency.

In fact, this seems to be the chosen recipe to escape the current debt burden of the U.S. economy. Growth/inflate your way out of it, which equates to running a high nominal GDP, which results in strong nominal earnings growth.

The dollar has been in a perpetual slide, and this also explains why equities keep rising higher. It’s a decent place to hide, though, so is Gold, which is also at an all-time high.

Indeed, if we price the S&P 500 in euros, for example, we are not at all-time highs.

Are stocks getting overvalued, or is the dollar simply becoming less valuable?

Passive Flows

This also ties into the next point, which is passive market flows.

Investing in markets has become incredibly common thanks to technology. And thanks to government policy, it has become increasingly profitable.

Everyone is beginning to understand that markets are the way to stay ahead of inflation and build meaningful wealth.

As such, so-called passive flows have begun to dominate the market.

Every month, billions flow into the market through 401(k)s and other instruments without regard to valuations or macroeconomic factors.

Big Tech

And this ties nicely with our last point, which is that big tech is leading and dominating the market.

First off, because big tech is actually leading the market in terms of EPS growth.

In turn, this makes them more valuable in the S&P 500, and this means that when passive flows move into the index, the Mag 7 gets even bigger.

Ultimately, this is a symptom of an economy that is built to support really big companies. Size is efficient, I guess, as long as it doesn't lead to a monopoly.

Final Thoughts

All in all, we have a market that generally won’t be allowed to dip too far, not just because it’s bad for politics, but also because it’s essentially the nation's retirement plan.

Better that the value of the dollar keep grinding lower.

Plus, as has been pointed out by many, though part of the economy seems weak, the big tech companies are still killing it, making this a very different beast than the 1999 bubble.

Of course, there are two factors that stop this train in its tracks:

  • An AI Capex Recession

  • Runaway inflation

More likely, it’s both. Inflation takes a toll on valuations, which in turn puts the brakes on animal spirits and makes AI financing more difficult.

Nonetheless, I still believe the next crash could be short-lived, for the reasons I’ve mentioned above.

Rather than try to time it, investors should understand that in the long term, the odds are stacked against cash, at least the dollar, and in favour of financial and real assets.

data.title: nCino: Revenue Growth Acceleration Should Happen In The Coming Years (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Summary

My previous investment thought on nCino Inc. (NASDAQ:NCNO) was an upgrade to a hold rating because there were early signs of a turnaround in mortgage churn, growing progress with winning large client deals, and a clear path to NCNO monetizing its AI capabilities. I upgrade NCNO to buy. AI Banking Advisor is gaining meaningful adoption and accelerating the shift to the new pricing model, while the mortgage business has flipped from headwind to tailwind. Add to this solid early traction in credit unions and a solid start to international expansion, and I believe the growth profile looks much stronger today.

AI And Platform Pricing

Previously, I pointed out NCNO AI targets as encouraging but ultimately thought it was a “show me story,” given that monetization was still in its infancy. This is no longer the case. The AI-powered Banking Advisor toolset has gone from an early proof-of-concept type of product to one that has gained real commercial traction, as evidenced by adoption from more than 80 customers. What makes this important is not just the revenue attached to Banking Advisor, but how AI is accelerating the transition—the customer base is moving to the new platform pricing model. We know this is happening since management has been clear that customer interest in Banking Advisor is pulling forward renewal conversations, which basically tells us this Banking Advisor AI is a powerful incentive for banks to migrate sooner than expected.

I see this as a game-changing dynamic. Previously, the timeline was a linear four-year renewal cycle, which meant the shift off the legacy seat-based model would be gradual, and so would the impact on the P&L. Now, AI is essentially compressing that timeline, allowing NCNO to realize the benefits of the new model much earlier in the P&L. Notably, the economics are extremely meaningful. On an apples-to-apples basis, management targets a 10% uplift in pricing as customers transition, plus a durable 2–3% annual tailwind from asset growth. My view is that this transition momentum will only get stronger from here because the AI Banking Advisor value proposition is simply too great. For perspective, the Banking Advisor reduces processes like credit memo narratives and mortgage data validation from 20–30 minutes down to seconds, which means employees can focus on higher-value tasks.

In terms of runway, with 21% of annual contract value [ACV] already transitioned, there is still substantial runway for NCNO to ride on this transition tailwind. This should help to drive meaningful growth acceleration, which should lift the stock sentiment.

Mortgage and Growth Initiatives

The mortgage business, which was a drag previously, has seen its turnaround progressing very nicely, and it is one that I think serves as another powerful tailwind to accelerate growth. For reference, in 2Q26, US mortgage subscription revenues grew 22% y/y to $20.9 million. This demand strength is particularly noteworthy given the soft macro backdrop—rates were still arguably high at high-6% in 2Q26. What also makes this so important is that the growth is entirely execution-driven. The growth came from higher volumes tied to large Independent Mortgage Banker [IMB] and homebuilder customers signed during the downturn, which suggests NCNO is taking meaningful market share (mortgage origination was down). Looking ahead, this tailwind should get stronger as the Feds have recently cut rates, and with mortgages down, refinancing activities have surged. This comes at a time when NCNO is transitioning to the new volume-based pricing model, which should help NCNO to capture more upside as activity returns. We also know that the momentum has continued through 3Q26 since management had the confidence to raise its FY26 outlook for the segment from flat to roughly 5% growth.

NCNO also has the right growth initiatives in place that serve as another growth accelerator. One of the most compelling ones is the push into the credit union market, which is made possible as NCNO’s consumer lending product has gotten a lot better. Consumer lending is an important aspect of credit union operations, as for credit unions, the ability to land with one solution like consumer or small business lending and then expand across their entire institution by standardizing on a single platform is a powerful value proposition. As NCNO management explained, a commercial client might apply for a car loan, and the bank "wouldn't have any idea who you are" because they are in a different system. By standardizing on a single platform like NCNO, a credit union can get a 360-degree view of their end customer, linking all their data in one place. The early traction is impressive, as NCNO has already seen six new logos and 35 cross-sells in 2Q25 alone, which is a very encouraging signal that NCNO offerings are well received and it is able to win share from incumbents.

Elsewhere, international execution, which NCNO has not executed well in the past, is also showing credible signs of improvement. The first win in Spain is particularly significant. I see this as a strategic beachhead, wherein by landing a customer (this is a non-bank lender), it validates the platform locally, and this gives NCNO “ammunition,” leverage, and references to accelerate growth in the region. As per management, continental Europe is a >$4 billion opportunity, so I am very positive about this win in Spain.

Valuation

I believe there is a good line of sight to NCNO accelerating revenue growth here, so long as it doesn’t mess up on executing the transition to the new pricing model. Management guided for ~9% y/y revenue growth in FY26; using that as a baseline, I forecast growth to accelerate by ~300 bps a year back to 15% by FY28, which is not a high bar since FY22-FY24 saw higher growth rates. Note that the AI Banking Advisors have compressed the timeline for transition, so we could see a sizeable chunk of growth coming online in the near term. As growth accelerates back to mid-teens, I think it makes sense for multiples to go back to FY24 levels as well. Assuming 6.2x forward revenue, I think the share price has a 40% upside from here.

Risk

Fundamentally, NCNO is dependent on Salesforce, with key solutions for client onboarding and loan origination built on Salesforce's technology. While their agreement extends through January 2031, any negative change in this relationship would materially impact the business. In addition, NCNO faces the risk of potential customer churn from consolidation in the financial industry and also more competition as NCNO expands into new verticals like consumer lending. Internally, the shift to a new asset-based pricing model carries the risk of disruptions.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for NCNO is now a buy. NCNO is no longer just a “show me story.” Its AI monetization, mortgage share gains, and new growth drivers should drive accelerating growth in the years to come. With growth poised to reaccelerate back to mid-teens and upside from the compressed pricing transition timeline, I see potential for a 40% share price gain from here.

data.title: 3 Monthly Dividend REITs That Could Set You Free
data.description:
Few investments provide as much and as reliable an income as real estate.

Your tenants pay rent checks on a monthly basis, resulting in highly consistent and predictable income that generally also rises over time.

But the downside of real estate is that it is not passive.

You first need to find the right property. Then you need to negotiate with the seller. Get financing. Do some renovations. Find a tenant. Manage the property. Etc.

It is a lot more work than most people realize.

Fortunately, there is a solution to earn a completely passive income from real estate, and that is through REITs (VNQ).

REITs are publicly listed real estate investment firms that allow you to invest in real estate just like how you would invest in any other industry - through the purchase of stock.

Moreover, REITs must pay out 90% of their taxable income to shareholders in the form of dividends, resulting in steady high income. While most pay their dividend quarterly, there are quite a few that pay on a monthly basis.

Finally, best of all, REITs are today trading at historically low valuations, which is resulting in relatively high dividend yields.

These days, my REIT investments generate enough monthly passive income for me to sustain myself.

In what follows, I will highlight three of my Top Picks today:

Agree Realty (ADC)

Agree Realty is a net lease REIT, just like Realty Income (O), targeting service-oriented properties such as grocery stores, quick-service restaurants, and home improvement stores.

Such properties earn highly consistent income because their lease terms are very long at over 10 years, annual rent escalations, and tenants are typically responsible for all property expenses, including even the maintenance.

But most investors ignore Agree Realty and will instead buy Realty Income because it is far bigger and better known. It also offers a higher yield and trades at a lower valuation:

Agree Realty Realty Income
P/FFO 16x 14x
Dividend Yield 4.3% 5.4%

I think that this is a mistake.

Yes, Agree Realty is priced at a premium and offers a lower yield, but there are good reasons for it.

It owns better assets, has lower leverage, and enjoys faster growth prospects, all of which justify a higher multiple.

Agree Realty Realty Income
Tenants with an investment-grade rating ~70% ~35%
Debt-to-EBITDA 3.1x 5.5x
Long-term expected annual growth 6% 4%

This has resulted in much higher returns over the past decade as the faster growth compounds over time:

Going forward, I expect the faster growth to more than make up for the lower yield, which should result in further outperformance over the long run.

The 4.3% dividend yield may not seem particularly high, but remember that the REIT is also retaining about 30% of its cash flow to reinvest in growth. Therefore, the real shareholder yield is 6.5%. A portion of that simply isn't paid out just yet. It is reinvested to increase the value of the company, which will eventually be reflected in a higher share price and dividend.

We expect about 5-6% annual growth over the long run, which should result in double-digit annual total returns.

Not bad coming from a highly defensive net lease investment with very low leverage.

Modiv Industrial (MDV)

I do not currently own a position in Modiv Industrial, but I have in the past and hold it high on my watchlist for a future potential investment.

The REIT makes a lot of sense for this list because Modiv stands for "Monthly Dividend".

Modiv is an industrial REIT that focuses on manufacturing net lease properties.

Just like Agree Realty, its lease terms are long, with 12 years left on them on average, resulting in highly consistent and predictable income. It also enjoys 2.7% average annual rent escalations, and its tenants are responsible for all property expenses.

Right now, there is a strong push towards greater manufacturing in the US, and the current administration is pushing for that with tariffs and other incentives.

This has the potential to increase the demand for Modiv's properties, and it may also result in additional investment opportunities for the REIT.

Historically, it has been able to buy properties at an 8%+ cap rate, and if the REIT can attain even a slightly higher valuation multiple, it could start raising new equity to accretively expand its portfolio.

Right now, its cost of equity is still too high, and it is slowing down its growth, but my prediction is that as interest rates return to lower levels, more capital will make its way to the REIT sector, and high-yielding REITs like Modiv will get bid up.

This will then allow it to greatly accelerate its future growth. While you wait, you are earning a near-8% dividend yield that's fully covered, and the management has made it their mission to pay reliable monthly income, which signals that they are very unlikely to cut it unless they really have to.

Whitestone REIT (WSR)

Whitestone REIT is the lowest-yielding REIT on this list, and yet, it might be my favorite opportunity because it offers the greatest potential for value creation and long-term upside.

The REIT owns primarily grocery-anchored, service-oriented strip centers in rapidly growing Sunbelt markets like Phoenix and Dallas.

These properties are today undersupplied, resulting in high occupancy rates and steady rent growth.

According to a recent study by Kimco Realty (KIM), rents would need to rise up 65% to make new development projects economically feasible in this sector due to the high construction cost.

Therefore, we expect the 3-5% same-property NOI growth to continue for many years to come. In addition to that, Whitestone is creating additional value by retaining about 40% of its cash flow to acquire additional properties and/or redevelop existing ones.

Its balance sheet is also gradually getting stronger as its cash flow increases, which will eventually justify a higher valuation multiple.

Even then, it is today trading at just 12x FFO and an estimated 30% discount to its net asset value. The REIT has received several offers from private equity companies to buy it out. The last offer was at $15, and the management argued that it undervalued the company.

Today, you can still buy shares at just below $13. While you wait for the upside, you earn a steady 4.2% dividend yield paid monthly.

Closing Note

Our Core Portfolio at High Yield Landlord is today offering a 6.2% average dividend yield, and it includes many monthly paying REITs. Best of all, we achieve this high yield with a low 59% average payout ratio, which leaves a lot of retained cash flow for the REITs to reinvest in growth.

data.title: Corporacion America Airports: Global Growth Story Still Priced As An Argentina Play
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In July, I published an article where I said that the biggest challenge for Corporación América Airports (NYSE:CAAP) was to get rid of the "Argentine risk premium" that the market applies almost by reflex. I argued that the company had ceased to be a simple emerging bet to become a global, profitable and diversified airport platform. Today, after the results of Q2 2025, I feel that this thesis has been strengthened: the fundamentals have been consolidated, but the market still does not recognize them in the valuation.

During the quarter, CAAP again grew in traffic, revenue and EBITDA, with margins that not only remained solid but also increased compared to last year. The most interesting thing is that he did it with a healthier balance sheet than ever: close to $500 MM in cash, total liquidity of $595 MM and a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of just 1.0x.

Despite this clear picture, the stock continues to be quoted with discounts of 40% to 60% compared to its peers. For me, that difference between what the numbers show and what the market discounts is the real entry point for the patient investor, so I maintain the "buy" stance.

Overview

Corporación América Airports (CAAP) operates 52 airports in six countries: Argentina, Italy, Uruguay, Brazil, Ecuador and Armenia. I think it is important to point out that this network gives it something that few operators have: the possibility of combining emerging markets with high growth potential with more mature ones that provide stability. That mix is what allows it to weather external shocks with a degree of resilience difficult to replicate.

In addition, CAAP has been increasingly focused on expanding non-aeronautical revenues, which is key to improving profitability. We saw it in Ezeiza, where the extension of the duty free was inaugurated in May, which went from 700 to 1,100 m2.

In Montevideo, work is developing to improve the passenger experience, and in Brazil, the shopping mall of Brasilia is started, with opening scheduled for 2026.

In Europe, the Florence master plan received environmental approval in April, and expansion of the Yerevan terminal continues in Armenia. All this confirms to me that CAAP sees its airports as commercial platforms and not just transit spaces.

The ability to finance these projects is explained by a very solid balance sheet. With $497 MM in cash at the close of Q2 and a total liquidity of $595 MM, the company can sustain long-term investments without compromising its financial structure.

As Martín Eurnekian, its CEO said,

We delivered a strong second quarter, with broad-based traffic growth, double-digit increases in revenue and Adjusted EBITDA, and meaningful EBITDA margin expansion, reflecting the strength of our diversified portfolio and disciplined execution"

Q2 2025

Q2 2025 made it clear that the air traffic recovery is no longer a rebound, but a new phase of growth. In Q2, CAAP transported 20.7 million passengers, an increase of 13.7% YoY, with historical records in Argentina, Italy, Armenia and Uruguay. Argentina was the big driver, with an increase of 17.3% YoY, but Italy, Brazil and Armenia also showed significant progress.

Consolidated revenue was $435.2 MM, up 18.9% YoY. When I look at the revenue per passenger, I see that it is $21, +4.5% YoY, which confirms that not only traffic is growing, but also the value added by each traveler, especially through commercial revenues. In fact, this line grew 22% YoY thanks to duty free, parking lots, VIP lounges and retail.

Adjusted EBITDA was $167.8MM, with a growth of 23.3% YoY and a margin of 38.6%, 140 bps above the previous year. Argentina led again with an EBITDA +34% YoY, but what stands out is that all markets, except Brazil, showed double-digit gains.

Net profit attributable to shareholders was $49.3 MM, stable compared to Q2 2024, mainly due to FX losses, although this does not affect the operating photo.

Valuation

What surprises me most is that, with this level of results, CAAP still trades at multiples that reflect excessive punishment. The EV/EBITDA FWD is at just 5.5x, less than half of the sector average at 12.6x. The EV/EBIT FWD stands at 7.7x against 17.5x in the industry.

Even the 11.9x P/E GAAP FWD implies a discount close to 50% compared to its peers. And if I look at the PEG FWD, I see 0.71x versus 1.83x of the sector: the market is not even recognizing the expected growth in earnings.

With the stock around $18.75 and a consensus price target of $24.6, I see an upside of the order of 30%. For me, this gap is not justified in the numbers and quality of assets that the company manages.

Growth

The growth profile of CAAP is another spread that, in my opinion, the market underestimates. In Q2, revenues grew 26.9% YoY, compared to just 3.6% of the sector average. Going forward, the consensus expects a 12.8% increase in revenues, more than double the 4.9% of the sector.

In EPS, CAAP projects an annual growth of 16.3%, almost double the 8.2% of industry. Even on a horizon of three to five years, EPS shows an increase of 16.7% CARG against 11.6% of the average. This confirms to me that we are not facing a one-off rebound, but a path of sustainable expansion. To this is added that FCF grew 37.8% YoY, reinforcing the company’s ability to finance its own growth without resorting to additional debt.

Profitability

If valuation and growth already look attractive, when I look at profitability CAAP directly stands out as one of the most efficient players in its universe. The EBITDA margin in Q2 was 34.2%, compared to 14.3% for the sector, which is more than double. The EBIT margin reached 22.4%, against 10.1% in industry.

The most notable is the FCF margin, which reached 22%, while the sectoral average barely exceeds 6.6%. This tells me that CAAP not only generates revenue, but turns it into cash exceptionally. The ROIC was 9.8%, compared to 7.0% of its peers, which confirms that it uses its capital more efficiently.

Risks

In spite of everything, it is clear that not everything is perfect. The point that I think is important to highlight is that CAAP’s exposure to Argentina remains high, as more than 60% of revenues still come from there. The risk of inflation and FX can generate mismatches between costs in pesos and dollar rates.

In addition, the airport business is always subject to external shocks, such as geopolitical crises or changes in the travel cycle.

But what reassures me is that CAAP has shown, even during the pandemic, that it knows how to adapt and preserve margins in adverse contexts.

Conclusion

Q2 2025 again showed that Corporación América Airports not only recovered what was lost after the pandemic, but emerged strengthened, with a more diversified network, expanding margins and a solid balance sheet. The current multiples reflect a punishment that no longer has support in the fundamentals.

While the market continues to look at it as an Argentine proxy, I see it for what it really is: a global, efficient and growing company. For me, that difference between perception and reality is exactly where the opportunity is, so my stance remains "buy".

data.title: American Battery Technology: Speculative Fervor As Trump Administration Considers Stake In Lithium Americas
data.description:
One of the most intense rallies of 2025 has been in the battery and power technology industry. The emerging field is made primarily of smaller, low- or pre-revenue companies that may have a growing total addressable market. In my view, this "industry" includes both companies pursuing new power generation technology, prominently small nuclear reactor firms like Oklo (OKLO), as well as storage technology companies like Solid Power (SLDP).

Although these two types of firms are not directly related, they're both benefiting from hype surrounding increased government support (lower permitting requirements or direct grants/loans), as well as very high demand from the utilities industry. The latter demand driver is rooted in forecasts of much higher US electricity demand from EVs and data centers over the next decade, following about two decades of no power demand growth.

Many stocks in the "battery and power technology" industry have doubled or more this year. To me, this raises the risk of overvaluation, particularly because stock prices are likely to rise on increased media exposure. As such, we must focus on revenue/income potential offset by valuations and risks that these technologies will not be as fruitful as hoped.
